questions.explanation: In the present simple, he/she/it makes the negative with doesn't + base verb: My sister doesn't like coffee. "Don't" is for I/you/we/they; isn't and aren't belong to the verb to be, not to an ordinary verb like like.

questions.prompt:
Reading — Read: "Sami works in a school. He helps children learn to read, and every evening he marks their homework."
What is most likely Sami's job?
questions.explanation: The text never says "teacher", but working in a school, helping children learn to read and marking homework together point to a teacher — a supported inference. A nurse helps sick people, a farmer grows crops, and a pilot flies planes, so none of them matches the school clues.
The error is (d): a noun ending consonant + y changes to -ies, so it is babies, never "babys". The correct ones follow the -es rule after -x, -s and -sh: boxes, buses, dishes are all right.

questions.prompt: Vocabulary — Which word is the odd one out?
questions.explanation: Doctor, teacher and farmer are all jobs, but Monday is a day of the week, so it is the odd one out. The trap is that Monday looks at home among capitalised words, but it belongs to a different set — sort by meaning, not by appearance.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Choose the correct article: "___ sun is very hot today."
questions.explanation: There is only one sun, so it is unique and takes the: The sun is very hot. We use a/an for one of many on first mention (a book), but a one-of-a-kind thing — the sun, the moon, the sky — always takes the.
The error is (b): city ends in consonant + y, so it becomes cities, not "citys". The correct ones end in vowel + y, which simply adds -s: keys, days, boys. The letter before the y decides the rule.
